Title: Editing VvDXS1 for the creation of muscat flavour in Vitis vinifera cv. Scarlet Royal

Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Native genomic locus edited by prime editing; no transgenic promoter used to drive VvDXS1 itself
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Plastidial DOXP/MEP pathway for terpene/monoterpene biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: MEP precursor-supply module for monoterpene biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: Thiamine diphosphate-dependent transketolase family; DXS enzyme
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Condenses pyruvate and D-glyceraldehyde 3-phosphate to form 1-deoxy-D-xylulose 5-phosphate, releasing CO2
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Monoterpenes associated with muscat flavour, including β-myrcene, linalool, citronellol, geraniol
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site: Leaves in this study; grape berries are discussed as muscat flavour organs in grape
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Volatile aroma compounds responsible for muscat flavour
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species: Transcribed in leaves; edited and non-edited alleles were transcribed similarly in transgenic vines
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Leaf tissues of transgenic vines were analyzed for genomic editing, transcript detection, and monoterpene accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as a major causal control point for muscat flavour; paper states VvDXS1 encodes the first enzyme and prior work identified the K284N mutation as the major cause for muscat flavour
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Editing the Lys284 allele to Asn284 increased leaf monoterpene accumulation and created a muscat-flavour-associated metabolic profile
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Prime editing converted the non-muscat VvDXS1 Lys284 allele to an engineered Asn284 muscat-like allele in Vitis vinifera cv. Scarlet Royal. 2. Edited vines accumulated several-fold higher levels of key muscat-associated monoterpenes, including β-myrcene, linalool, citronellol, and geraniol, than unedited controls. 3. The edited allele was transcribed similarly to the wild-type allele, and high-editing lines showed the strongest monoterpene increases.
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Prime editing of endogenous locus; PCR/AclI genotyping; Sanger sequencing; amplicon sequencing for editing efficiency; RT-PCR/cDNA sequencing for transcript validation; GC-MS metabolite profiling of monoterpenes; clonal propagation analysis
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Amplicon sequencing of edited target region; metabolite profiling by GC-MS; data in supplementary tables, no public accession reported
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Greenhouse growth under 16 h light/8 h dark at 26°C for transgenic vines; comparison with field-grown muscat and non-muscat cultivars
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: High-value target for precise editing to create or enhance muscat flavour in elite grape cultivars, including converting non-muscat alleles to muscat alleles and strengthening flavour in heterozygous muscat varieties
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: No obvious phenotypic difference in vine growth or development was observed; main limitation was low editing efficiency and chimerism
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The causal edited amino acid change is Lys284Asn; engineered codon was AAC to introduce an AclI marker site. Natural muscat cultivars commonly carry Asn284. High-fidelity editing with few indels or unintended nearby mutations was reported.
